planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in shade to part shade; Red ivy is a low, spreading tropical Southeast Asian groundcover grown for its distinctive leaves — metallic dark green-gray with a purple reverse and a wrinkled, waffle-textured surface — it is one of the finest shade groundcovers for tropical gardens and performs well in containers and hanging baskets.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to part shade; keep consistently moist for best foliage color. Hemigraphis alternata (red ivy or waffle plant) is a low-growing, creeping tropical perennial from Java and other parts of Southeast Asia in the acanthus family (Acanthaceae), grown primarily as an ornamental for the distinctive foliage: the leaves are oval to heart-shaped, with a deeply wrinkled, puckered, waffle-like texture on the upper surface (hence 'waffle plant') and are metallic dark greenish-gray with silver highlights on top, contrasting with a vivid, wine-red to magenta-purple underside. The undersurface color gives the plant its other common name, 'red ivy,' especially as it spreads along the ground and the underside is visible. Stems root readily at the nodes, allowing the plant to spread as a groundcover in warm, humid conditions. Small, white, tubular flowers are produced but are inconspicuous — the foliage is the feature. As a shade container or hanging basket plant, red ivy is outstanding: the trailing stems with metallic-textured leaves create an elegant, unusual display. In zone 10–12, it functions as a year-round shade groundcover. It is also popular as a tropical aquarium plant and terrarium subject. One of the most distinctive-looking low groundcovers for warm, humid shade.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Groundcovers

common_name: Japanese Sweet Flag (Dwarf Sweet Flag, Grassy-Leaved Sweet Flag)

planting.note: Plant clump divisions in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil or in containers submerged up to 2–3 inches of water in shade to part shade; Japanese sweet flag is a small, grass-like aquatic-marginal perennial from East Asia — the mildly anise-scented leaves are used in traditional Japanese ikebana arrangements, as a marginal water feature plant, and in various ornamental cultivars with variegated gold-and-green foliage.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil or in containers in shallow water (up to 3 inches deep) in shade to part shade. Acorus gramineus (Japanese sweet flag or dwarf sweet flag) is a small, compact, grass-like aquatic-marginal perennial from Japan and China — a diminutive relative of the larger sweet flag (A. calamus) — producing dense tufts of narrow, grass-like, slightly arching, aromatic leaves in solid green or with variegated golden-yellow striping. The leaves, when crushed, release a mild, pleasant anise-cinnamon fragrance similar to (but softer than) the larger A. calamus. Several ornamental cultivars are widely grown: 'Ogon' (yellow-green striped leaves), 'Variegatus' (white-striped leaves), and the miniature 'Pusillus' (just 3–4 inches tall). Japanese sweet flag naturally grows along stream margins and in wet meadows in Japan and Korea, tolerating conditions from moist soil to shallow water up to 3 inches deep; it is an ideal marginal plant for water garden pots, pond edges, and shade rain gardens. In Japanese flower arranging (ikebana), the upright leaves provide linear structure. In Chinese and Japanese traditional medicine, the root and leaves are used as a mildly stimulating tonic and insect repellent herb. A wonderfully versatile plant for moist shade situations.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Water Garden Plants

common_name: Big Blue Lilyturf (Border Lilyturf, Muscari Liriope)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to full sun; Big Blue lilyturf is the clumping (non-spreading) species of liriope — one of the most widely used shade groundcovers in the American South, with upright to arching, dark-green, grass-like foliage, summer spikes of purple flowers, and persistent black berries — virtually indestructible once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to full sun; shear to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges. Liriope muscari (big blue lilyturf or border lilyturf) is one of the most widely planted and reliably performing shade groundcovers and border plants in the American South and Gulf Coast — a clumping (not spreading) species distinct from creeping lilyturf (L. spicata). The plant produces dense, arching clumps of dark-green, grass-like evergreen leaves 12–18 inches long, topped in summer with upright spikes of small, purple to violet flower clusters resembling grape hyacinth (muscari — hence 'muscari' in the name) rising above the foliage. The flowers are followed by persistent, shiny, black berry-like fruits that provide additional ornamental interest through fall and winter and are eaten by birds. Named cultivars are numerous and widely available: 'Big Blue' (classic standard); 'Monroe White' (white flowers); 'Gold Band' (yellow-striped leaves); 'Silvery Sunproof' (cream-striped, more sun-tolerant). Liriope muscari is extraordinarily adaptable in the Gulf South: it tolerates deep shade, drought, poor soils, salt spray, and competition from tree roots — performing where few other groundcovers survive. Shear clumps to the ground in late winter (use a lawnmower) to remove tattered old foliage before fresh new growth emerges in spring.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Liriope
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade-Tolerant Groundcovers

common_name: Woman's Tongue (Siris Tree, East Indian Walnut)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Woman's Tongue is a fast-growing, nitrogen-fixing, tropical legume shade tree famous for its persistent, flat, papery seed pods that rattle noisily in the wind (hence the evocative common name) — CAUTION: classified as a Category I invasive exotic in Florida where it naturalizes aggressively in disturbed areas and natural habitats.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; extremely drought-tolerant once established. CAUTION: invasive in Florida — do not plant near natural areas. Albizia lebbeck (woman's tongue or siris tree) is a large, fast-growing, deciduous-to-semi-evergreen tropical legume tree native to South Asia and tropical Africa, now widely naturalized throughout the tropics as both a valued shade tree and an invasive species. The plant is named 'woman's tongue' for its persistent, flat, tan, papery, 6–10 inch long seed pods that hold their seeds loosely and rattle loudly in the slightest breeze — the metaphorical association being obvious. As a landscape tree, Woman's Tongue is excellent: it grows rapidly (3–5 feet per year), provides dense, spreading shade, fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root bacteria, produces attractive cream-white, fragrant, powder-puff flower clusters in late spring, and is extraordinarily drought-tolerant on poor soils. The bark has traditionally yielded saponins used as a soap substitute in India. The wood is highly valued as a timber for furniture and construction in South Asia. IMPORTANT CAUTION: In Florida, coastal Gulf South states, and Hawaii, Albizia lebbeck is classified as an invasive exotic pest plant — it naturalizes in disturbed areas, roadsides, and natural areas aggressively. Do not plant in proximity to natural lands in Florida or coastal Gulf states.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Invasive Plants
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Albizia lebbeck

common_name: Variegated Thyme (Silver Posie Thyme, Gold Thyme)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; Variegated thyme provides the same culinary uses as common English thyme but with striking ornamental appeal — silvery-white-edged leaves ('Silver Posie' type) or golden-yellow-edged leaves ('Gold' type) that brighten herb gardens, rock gardens, and container plantings while still providing fully flavored thyme for the kitchen.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; clip lightly after flowering to maintain compact mound shape. Thymus vulgaris 'Variegatus' (variegated thyme) encompasses several cultivated varieties of common garden thyme selected for ornamental variegated foliage rather than just culinary use. The two primary types are: silver-variegated ('Silver Posie,' 'Silver Queen') with small, dark-green leaves edged in silver-white, creating a soft, frosted effect; and gold-variegated ('Gold,' 'Aureus') with leaves edged in yellow-gold, brightening the herb garden considerably. Both retain the characteristic thyme fragrance and flavor of common thyme (Thymus vulgaris) and can be used identically in cooking — the variegated leaves look beautiful in cooking and as garnishes. Variegated thyme cultivars tend to be slightly less vigorous than solid-green common thyme but grow readily under the same cultural conditions: excellent drainage, lean soil, hot sun, and minimal water. They are especially effective in herb garden borders, rock gardens, and as edging plants where the contrast between the dark green leaf centers and the cream, silver, or gold margins can be appreciated at close range. Also excellent in containers or topiaries. In hot, humid zone 9 climates, variegated thyme benefits from excellent air circulation to prevent fungal problems.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Culinary Herbs

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich soil in part shade to full sun; Wood calamint is a European woodland herb of the mint family with leaves that smell and taste like a combination of basil and thyme — the 'basil thyme' common name is apt — it is used in Italian cooking, particularly in Umbria and Lazio (where it is known as 'nepitella'), as a seasoning for artichokes, mushrooms, and tripe.

directions: Sow seeds or plant divisions in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; harvest leaves as needed before flowering for best flavor. Calamintha sylvatica (wood calamint or basil thyme) is a European woodland herb in the mint family (Lamiaceae), native to the forests, hedgerows, and shaded rocky slopes of southern and central Europe — with a particularly close association with Italian cuisine. The leaves have a distinctive aroma and flavor that has been aptly described as a synthesis of fresh basil and thyme with additional minty notes — more complex and interesting than either herb alone. In Italian cooking, this herb is known as 'nepitella' (or 'mentuccia' in Lazio) — an important and distinctly Italian culinary herb with a long tradition in Roman and Umbrian cooking. It is a classic pairing for artichokes (in Roman-style carciofi alla romana), porcini mushrooms, tripe (trippa alla Romana), and game dishes; the herb adds a fresh, aromatic complexity that is genuinely distinct from standard basil or thyme. Wood calamint is notably more shade-tolerant than most herbs — it grows well at the edge of light shade under trees, making it useful for herb gardens with limited full-sun space. The small, white to pale-lavender flowers attract bees prolifically and the plant is excellent for pollinator gardens.
sources: University of Rome La Sapienza — Italian Culinary Herbs

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; edible amaranth (tampala) is an Asian and tropical leafy vegetable amaranth grown for its exceptionally nutritious, mild-flavored leaves — it is the primary amaranth vegetable of South and Southeast Asian cuisine, used in stir-fries, curries, and soups in China, India, the Philippines, and throughout tropical Asia.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in warm, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; harvest outer leaves from 30 days; pull entire plant at 50–60 days before flowering. Amaranthus tricolor (edible amaranth or tampala) is a South and Southeast Asian edible leafy vegetable that is one of the most important leaf vegetables in tropical Asian cooking — the primary amaranth grown for its edible leaves (as distinct from grain amaranths like A. cruentus). The plant produces large, oval, slightly crinkled leaves in spectacular color combinations ranging from solid green or red-tinged green to dramatic tricolor varieties with patches and blotches of red, gold, and green or entirely deep crimson to magenta — the basis for the ornamental varieties sold as 'Joseph's Coat' in Western gardens. The leaves are nutritionally outstanding: very high in protein (15–20% on a dry-weight basis), iron, calcium, and vitamins A and C — one of the most nutrient-dense leafy vegetables. In Chinese cooking (where it is called 'xian cai'), the leaves are stir-fried with garlic; in Indian cooking (used as 'chaulai' in Hindi), the leaves are sauteed with spices; throughout Southeast Asia, it is used in soups and curries as a heat-tolerant spinach substitute. The heat tolerance of tampala — thriving in temperatures that cause spinach and other cool-season greens to bolt — makes it especially valuable in Gulf Coast and tropical summer gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Leafy Greens
